properties.definition: Official title of the resilience plan document as published by the authoring organization.
examples: Planning and Policies for Resilience in Dar es Salaam
properties.notes: Primary human-readable identifier for the plan. Should be stored exactly as published, including subtitle if part of official title.

properties.definition: Classification of the plan document indicating its primary focus area or strategic purpose.
properties.notes: Consider creating controlled vocabulary for common types. Enables filtering and comparison of similar plan types across jurisdictions.

properties.definition: Year in which the plan was officially adopted, approved, or published by the responsible authority.
properties.notes: High coverage (90%) and critical for temporal analysis. Store as integer year only. If only month/year available, use year.

properties.definition: Number of years from adoption that the plan is designed to cover, representing the intended implementation timeframe.
properties.notes: 74% coverage. Useful for calculating target_year (adoption_year + planning_horizon_years). Wide variance suggests different planning approaches (short-term tactical vs long-term strategic).

properties.definition: Total count of discrete actions, initiatives, or interventions specified within the plan document.
properties.notes: Only 42% coverage but valuable for plan scope analysis. May be derived from Action entity count if actions are modeled separately in graph.

properties.definition: Budget or financial resources mentioned in the plan, captured as free text due to varying formats and contexts.
properties.notes: Low coverage (14%) and highly inconsistent format (past spending vs future budget, different currencies, different scopes). Store as string; consider separate structured currency fields if standardization needed.

properties.definition: Description of how the plan's implementation and outcomes will be monitored, evaluated, or reported over time.
examples: Monitoring and Evaluation section mentioned in Chapter 5 with focus on mainstreaming
High-quality internal and external environmental reporting and communications
Review/update strategies taking into account scientific knowledge, progress of implementation
properties.notes: 78% coverage. Free text field capturing M&E methodology. Consider extracting structured monitoring_frequency or reporting_mechanism properties if patterns emerge.

properties.definition: Unique resource identifier or persistent URL where the plan document can be accessed.
properties.notes: Not in source data but recommended for linking to external resources and ensuring global uniqueness in federated knowledge graphs.

properties.definition: Direct URL to the published PDF or web version of the plan document.
properties.notes: Not in source data but critical for provenance and verification. Store multiple URLs if plan available in multiple formats.

properties.definition: Geographic area or jurisdiction covered by the plan (city, region, state, etc).
properties.notes: Not explicitly in source data but derivable from plan_title. Should link to Location entity rather than store as string property.

properties.definition: Current status of the plan in its lifecycle (draft, adopted, active, completed, superseded).
properties.notes: Not in source data but important for temporal queries. Consider controlled vocabulary: draft, adopted, active, under_review, completed, superseded, cancelled.

relationships.definition: Organization that created, wrote, or commissioned the development of the plan document.
relationships.notes: Source from authoring_organizations array (100% coverage). Multiple organizations often collaborate on plan development. Consider adding role property to distinguish lead author from contributors.

relationships.definition: Agency, department, or organization responsible for executing or overseeing implementation of the plan.
relationships.notes: Source from implementing_agencies array (96% coverage). May overlap with AUTHORED_BY but represents operational responsibility. Useful for accountability mapping.

relationships.definition: External framework, standard, or methodology that the plan explicitly references or aligns with.
relationships.notes: Source from external_frameworks array (96% coverage). Examples: 100RC City Resilience Framework, SDGs, Sendai Framework. Enables cross-plan comparison by framework adoption.

relationships.definition: Previous version or earlier plan that this plan replaces or updates.
relationships.notes: Not in source data but critical for tracking plan evolution over time. Enables version history queries and longitudinal analysis of changing priorities.

relationships.definition: Newer version or updated plan that replaces this plan.
relationships.notes: Inverse of SUPERSEDES relationship. Useful for navigating to current version from historical plans.

relationships.definition: Geographic area, jurisdiction, or place that the plan addresses.
relationships.notes: Derivable from plan titles and context. Location entity should include city, region, country hierarchy. Enables geographic queries and spatial analysis.

relationships.definition: Specific action, initiative, or intervention defined within the plan.
relationships.notes: If actions are modeled as separate entities, this relationship enables drilling down from plan to implementation details. Count of these relationships should match total_actions property.

relationships.definition: Climate hazard, risk, or threat that the plan is designed to address or mitigate.
relationships.notes: Not in source data but critical for risk-based queries. Examples: flooding, heat waves, drought, sea level rise. Enables finding all plans addressing specific hazards.

relationships.definition: Economic or social sector that the plan focuses on or impacts.
relationships.notes: Not in source data but valuable for sector-specific analysis. Examples: transportation, energy, water, health, housing. Enables cross-sectoral resilience queries.

relationships.definition: Another plan that this plan explicitly cites, builds upon, or coordinates with.
relationships.notes: Not in source data but important for understanding plan ecosystems. Enables network analysis of how plans reference and influence each other across jurisdictions.

relationships.definition: Organization or funding body that provided financial resources for plan development or implementation.
relationships.notes: Not explicitly in source data but may be derivable from authoring_organizations or document text. Important for understanding resource flows and donor influence.
